Subject: Chemistry
title: Acids, Bases & pH
content:
• Acids: Sour taste, turn Blue litmus Red, pH < 7.
  • Bases: Bitter taste, soapy feel, turn Red litmus Blue, pH > 7.
  • pH Scale: Measures H+ concentration. 7 is Neutral (Pure Water).
  • Neutralization: Acid + Base → Salt + Water.

Subject: Biology
title: Nervous System & Hormones
content:
• Neurons: The structural unit of the nervous system. Signals cross at the Synapse.
  • Reflex Action: Sudden response to stimuli via the Reflex Arc in the spinal cord.
  • Plant Hormones: Auxin (Growth), Cytokinin (Cell division), Abscisic acid (Inhibits growth).
  • Human Brain: Cerebrum (Thinking), Cerebellum (Balance), Medulla (Involuntary actions).

Subject: Physics
title: Electricity Essentials
content:
• Ohm's Law: V = IR (Voltage = Current × Resistance).
  • Series Circuits: Current is the same; Resistance adds up (R1 + R2).
  • Parallel Circuits: Voltage is the same; 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2.
  • Power: P = VI = I²R = V²/R.

Subject: Chemistry
title: Reaction Quick Guide
content:
• Combination: Two reactants → One product (A+B→AB).
  • Decomposition: One reactant → Two products (AB→A+B).
  • Displacement: More reactive element replaces a less reactive one.
  • Exothermic: Heat is released (feels hot).

Subject: Biology
title: Biology: Life Processes
content:
• Nutrition: Autotrophs (Plants) use Photosynthesis; Heterotrophs (Humans) consume others.
  • Respiration: Breakdown of food to release energy (ATP). Happens in Mitochondria.
  • Transportation: Blood in humans, Xylem/Phloem in plants.
  • Excretion: Removal of toxic wastes via Kidneys (Nephrons).

Subject: Mathematics
title: Maths: Quadratic Formula
content:
• Standard Form: ax² + bx + c = 0
  • Discriminant (D): b² - 4ac.
  • Nature of Roots:
    - If D > 0: 2 real & distinct roots.
    - If D = 0: 2 real & equal roots.
    - If D < 0: No real roots.

Subject: Physics
title: Mastering Refraction & Lenses
content:
• Refraction: The bending of light as it passes from one medium to another.
  • Speed of Light: Light bends towards the normal when entering a denser medium (slows down).
  • Snell's Law: sin(i) / sin(r) = constant (Refractive Index).
  • Lenses: Convex lenses converge light; Concave lenses diverge light.

question_text: When a ray of light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, it:

explanations.basic: Light slows down when entering a denser medium, causing it to bend towards the normal line.
explanations.advanced: According to Snell's law (n1*sin(i) = n2*sin(r)), if n2 > n1 (rarer to denser), then sin(r) must be less than sin(i), meaning angle r is less than angle i, so it bends towards the normal.

Subject: Physics

question_text: The refractive index of water is 1.33 and that of glass is 1.5. If light travels from water to glass, it will:

explanations.basic: Glass (1.5) has a higher refractive index than water (1.33), so glass is optically denser. Light traveling from rarer (water) to denser (glass) bends towards the normal.
explanations.advanced: The relative refractive index of glass with respect to water is n_gw = n_g / n_w = 1.5 / 1.33 ≈ 1.13. Since n_gw > 1, the ray bends towards the normal.

Subject: Physics

question_text: A convex lens forms a real and inverted image of the same size as the object when the object is placed:

explanations.basic: For a convex lens, an object placed at 2F (twice the focal length) produces a real, inverted image exactly at 2F on the other side, and of the same size.
explanations.advanced: Using the lens formula 1/v - 1/u = 1/f. If u = -2f, then 1/v - (1/-2f) = 1/f => 1/v = 1/f - 1/2f = 1/2f. So v = +2f. Magnification m = v/u = (2f)/(-2f) = -1. The magnitude 1 means same size, and the negative sign means inverted.

Subject: Physics

question_text: Twinkling of stars is primarily due to:

explanations.basic: As starlight enters the Earth's atmosphere, it undergoes continuous refraction because the atmosphere has layers of different optical densities.
explanations.advanced: The Earth's atmosphere has varying physical conditions (temperature and density), which continuously change its refractive index. The starlight path fluctuates, causing the apparent position of the star to change slightly and the amount of starlight entering the eye to flicker, which we perceive as twinkling.

Subject: Physics

question_text: The law of refraction stating that the ratio of sine of angle of incidence to the sine of angle of refraction is constant is called:

explanations.basic: This relationship (sin i / sin r = constant) was discovered by Willebrord Snellius and is known as Snell's law of refraction.
explanations.advanced: Snell's Law can be mathematically expressed as n1 * sin(θ1) = n2 * sin(θ2). The constant in the basic formulation (sin i / sin r = n) is the relative refractive index of the second medium with respect to the first.

Subject: Physics

question_text: Which of the following materials cannot be used to make a lens?

explanations.basic: A lens must be made of a transparent material so that light can pass through it and refract. Clay is opaque.
explanations.advanced: A functional lens requires a material with a consistent refractive index that allows high transmission of visible light. Clay is non-homogeneous and opaque, meaning it completely absorbs or reflects visible light, preventing any controlled refraction.

Subject: Physics

question_text: If the magnification produced by a lens has a negative value, the image is:

explanations.basic: A negative magnification means the image is flipped upside down (inverted). Inverted images formed by lenses are always real images.
explanations.advanced: Magnification m = h'/h. Since the object height h is conventionally taken as positive (above the principal axis), a negative m implies the image height h' is negative (below the principal axis). For lenses, images formed below the principal axis are real and inverted.

Subject: Physics

question_text: The power of a lens is -2.0 D. The lens is:

options.A: Convex lens of focal length 50 cm
options.B: Concave lens of focal length 50 cm
options.C: Convex lens of focal length 20 cm
options.D: Concave lens of focal length 20 cm

explanations.basic: Power is negative, so it's a concave lens. Focal length f = 1/Power. f = 1/-2.0 = -0.5 meters. 0.5 meters is 50 cm.
explanations.advanced: Power P = 1/f (in meters). P = -2.0 D means f = -0.5 m = -50 cm. The negative sign conventionally denotes a diverging (concave) lens. This lens would be prescribed for someone suffering from myopia (near-sightedness).

Subject: Physics

question_text: An object is placed at 20 cm from a convex lens of focal length 10 cm. The image is formed at:

explanations.basic: The focal length is 10 cm, so 20 cm is exactly twice the focal length (2F). For a convex lens, an object at 2F forms an image at 2F on the other side, which is also 20 cm away.
explanations.advanced: Applying 1/v - 1/u = 1/f with u = -20 cm, f = +10 cm. 1/v - (1/-20) = 1/10 => 1/v + 1/20 = 1/10 => 1/v = 1/10 - 1/20 = 1/20. Therefore v = +20 cm, proving the image forms at 20 cm on the opposite side.

Subject: Physics

question_text: The speed of light in a transparent medium is 2 x 10^8 m/s. The refractive index of the medium is (speed of light in vacuum = 3 x 10^8 m/s):

explanations.basic: Refractive index = (Speed of light in vacuum) / (Speed of light in medium). So, (3 x 10^8) / (2 x 10^8) = 3/2 = 1.5.
explanations.advanced: Absolute refractive index (n) measures how much a medium slows down light relative to a vacuum. n = c/v. Here n = (3.0 * 10^8 m/s) / (2.0 * 10^8 m/s) = 1.5. This is typically the refractive index of glass.

Subject: Physics

question_text: According to Ohm's law, if the resistance of a conductor is doubled while keeping the potential difference constant, the current will become:

explanations.basic: Since V = IR, if V is constant and R is doubled (2R), the current I must become half (I/2) to balance the equation.
explanations.advanced: I = V/R. If R' = 2R, then I' = V/(2R) = 1/2 * (V/R) = 1/2 * I. This demonstrates the inverse relationship between current and resistance.

Subject: Physics

question_text: Which of the following represents the correct formula for Electric Power (P) in terms of Voltage (V) and Resistance (R)?

explanations.basic: Electric power can be calculated using the square of the voltage divided by the resistance (V²/R).
explanations.advanced: Starting from P = VI and substituting I = V/R from Ohm's law, we get P = V * (V/R) = V²/R. This formula is useful when the voltage is constant.

Subject: Physics

question_text: A wire of resistance R is cut into five equal pieces. These pieces are then connected in parallel. If the equivalent resistance of this combination is R', then the ratio R/R' is:

explanations.basic: Cutting the wire makes each piece R/5. Connecting 5 such pieces in parallel makes the total resistance (R/5)/5 = R/25. The ratio R / (R/25) is 25.
explanations.advanced: Each piece has resistance r = R/5. In parallel, 1/R' = 1/r + 1/r + 1/r + 1/r + 1/r = 5/r = 5 / (R/5) = 25/R. Therefore, R/R' = 25.

Subject: Physics

question_text: When calcium oxide reacts vigorously with water to form calcium hydroxide, it is an example of:

explanations.basic: Two reactants (CaO and H2O) combine to form a single product [Ca(OH)2], which is a combination reaction.
explanations.advanced: CaO(s) + H2O(l) → Ca(OH)2(aq) + Heat. This is also an exothermic reaction as a large amount of heat is released.

Subject: Chemistry

question_text: Which of the following is a physical change?

explanations.basic: Melting of ice is a physical change because only the state of water changes (solid to liquid), and no new chemical substance is formed.
explanations.advanced: Physical changes involve changes in physical properties like state, shape, or size without altering the molecular composition. Burning, rusting, and cooking are chemical changes as they involve chemical reactions and formation of new substances.

Subject: Chemistry

question_text: The brown gas evolved during the thermal decomposition of lead nitrate is:

explanations.basic: When lead nitrate is heated, it breaks down and releases nitrogen dioxide, which is a reddish-brown gas.
explanations.advanced: 2Pb(NO3)2(s) → 2PbO(s) + 4NO2(g) + O2(g). The NO2 gas is responsible for the characteristic brown fumes observed during this decomposition reaction.

Subject: Chemistry

question_text: A student adds iron nails to a solution of copper sulphate. After some time, the blue color of the solution fades and a reddish-brown deposit is seen. This is a:

explanations.basic: Iron is more reactive than copper, so it displaces copper from the copper sulphate solution. This is a displacement reaction.
explanations.advanced: Fe(s) + CuSO4(aq) → FeSO4(aq) + Cu(s). The blue color of CuSO4 fades as green FeSO4 forms, and the reddish-brown deposit is the displaced copper metal.

Subject: Chemistry

question_text: The pH of a neutral solution at 25°C is:

explanations.basic: On the pH scale, a value of 7 indicates a neutral solution (neither acidic nor basic).
explanations.advanced: pH is defined as -log10[H+]. At 25°C, pure water has [H+] = 10^-7 mol/L, thus pH = 7.

Subject: Chemistry

question_text: When a drop of phenolphthalein is added to an acidic solution, the color of the solution becomes:

explanations.basic: Phenolphthalein is a colorless indicator that stays colorless in acidic solutions but turns pink in basic solutions.
explanations.advanced: Phenolphthalein is a weak acid itself. In acidic environments (high H+ concentration), the equilibrium shifts towards the non-ionized colorless form.

Subject: Chemistry

question_text: The gas evolved when sodium carbonate reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid is:

explanations.basic: Reacting a metal carbonate with an acid always produces carbon dioxide gas, which turns lime water milky.
explanations.advanced: Na2CO3(s) + 2HCl(aq) → 2NaCl(aq) + H2O(l) + CO2(g). The evolution of CO2 with brisk effervescence is a characteristic test for carbonates.

Subject: Chemistry

question_text: What is the chemical formula of Plaster of Paris?

explanations.basic: Plaster of Paris is calcium sulphate hemihydrate, meaning it has half a molecule of water for every calcium sulphate molecule.
explanations.advanced: Plaster of Paris (CaSO4 · 1/2H2O) is obtained by heating Gypsum (CaSO4 · 2H2O) at 373 K. It has the property of setting into a hard mass when mixed with water.

Subject: Chemistry

question_text: The process by which green plants make their own food is called:

explanations.basic: Green plants use sunlight, water, and CO2 to create food (glucose) in a process called photosynthesis.
explanations.advanced: 6CO2 + 12H2O + Light → C6H12O6 + 6O2 + 6H2O. This takes place in the chloroplasts of the leaf cells.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Which chamber of the human heart receives oxygenated blood from the lungs?

explanations.basic: Oxygen-rich blood comes from the lungs and enters the Left Atrium of the heart first.
explanations.advanced: The pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium. From there, it moves to the left ventricle to be pumped to the rest of the body.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The breakdown of pyruvate to give carbon dioxide, water and energy takes place in:

explanations.basic: While the first step of breaking down glucose happens in the cytoplasm, the final energy release happens in the mitochondria (the powerhouse of the cell).
explanations.advanced: Aerobic respiration involves the oxidation of pyruvate in the mitochondria. This process produces significantly more ATP than anaerobic respiration.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The gap between two neurons is called a:

explanations.basic: Information travels across the tiny gap between two nerve cells called a synapse.
explanations.advanced: At the synapse, electrical signals are converted into chemical signals (neurotransmitters) to cross the gap and reach the next neuron.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Which plant hormone is primarily responsible for the growth of the stem?

explanations.basic: Gibberellins are the main hormones that help in the growth and elongation of the stem in plants.
explanations.advanced: Gibberellins promote cell division and cell elongation in stems. Auxins also help, but their primary role is apical dominance and phototropism.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The part of the brain responsible for maintaining posture and balance of the body is:

explanations.basic: The cerebellum (located at the back of the brain) is in charge of your balance, posture, and precise movements like cycling.
explanations.advanced: The cerebellum is part of the hindbrain. It coordinates voluntary movements such as posture, balance, coordination, and speech, resulting in smooth and balanced muscular activity.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The functional unit of the human kidney is called:

explanations.basic: Nephrons are tiny filtering units in the kidneys that remove waste from the blood.
explanations.advanced: Each kidney contains about 1 million nephrons. They consist of a renal corpuscle (Glomerulus and Bowman's capsule) and a renal tubule.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The near point of a normal human eye is at a distance of:

explanations.basic: For a normal adult eye, the closest distance at which objects can be seen clearly without strain is 25 cm.
explanations.advanced: The near point is the point at which the ciliary muscles are at their maximum contraction to increase the curvature of the lens for focusing close objects.

Subject: Science

question_text: If the discriminant of a quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 is greater than zero, the roots are:

explanations.basic: If D > 0, the equation has two different (distinct) real numbers as its solutions.
explanations.advanced: The discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac determines the nature of roots. D > 0 implies two distinct real roots, D = 0 implies two equal real roots, and D < 0 implies no real roots.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: Find the roots of the quadratic equation x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0.

explanations.basic: We need to find two numbers that multiply to 6 and add up to -5. Those numbers are -2 and -3. So (x-2)(x-3) = 0, which gives x = 2 or 3.
explanations.advanced: Using factorization: x^2 - 3x - 2x + 6 = 0 => x(x-3) - 2(x-3) = 0 => (x-2)(x-3) = 0. Therefore, the roots are 2 and 3.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: The values of k for which the quadratic equation 2x^2 + kx + 3 = 0 has two equal roots is:

explanations.basic: For equal roots, the discriminant (b^2 - 4ac) must be zero. Here, k^2 - 4(2)(3) = 0, so k^2 = 24.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: Which of the following mirrors is used by a dentist to examine a small cavity?

explanations.basic: A concave mirror forms a magnified, erect image of an object placed close to it, helping dentists see clearly.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

options.A: Convex lens of focal length 50 cm

options.C: Convex lens of focal length 20 cm

title: Sources of Energy Summary
content:
• Renewable vs Non-Renewable: Renewable sources (Solar, Wind, Hydro) can be replenished. Non-renewable (Coal, Petroleum) will run out.
  • Fossil Fuels: Formed from ancient biomass. Burning them causes pollution and global warming.
  • Biogas: A clean fuel produced by anaerobic decomposition. Mainly methane.
  • Solar Energy: Solar cells convert sunlight directly into electricity using silicon.

question_text: Which of the following is a renewable source of energy?

question_text: In a right-angled triangle $ABC$, right-angled at $B$, if $\sin A = \frac{3}{4}$, then $\cos A$ is:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Given $\sin A = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{3}{4}$, let opposite side $BC = 3k$ and hypotenuse $AC = 4k$.
Step 2: Use Pythagoras theorem $AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 \implies AB^2 + (3k)^2 = (4k)^2 \implies AB^2 = 16k^2 - 9k^2 = 7k^2$. So, $AB = \sqrt{7}k$.
Step 3: $\cos A = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{AB}{AC} = \frac{\sqrt{7}k}{4k} = \frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: What is the value of $(\sin^2 30^\circ + \cos^2 30^\circ)$?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Substitute the values $\sin 30^\circ = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\cos 30^\circ = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
Step 2: Calculate $(\frac{1}{2})^2 + (\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2})^2 = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1$.
Step 3: Alternatively, recall the trigonometric identity $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$ for any angle $\theta$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: If $\tan A = 1$, then the value of $2\sin A \cos A$ is:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: If $\tan A = 1$, then $A = 45^\circ$.
Step 2: Substitute $A = 45^\circ$ into the expression: $2\sin 45^\circ \cos 45^\circ = 2(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}})(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}})$.
Step 3: $2 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: The value of $\frac{1 - \tan^2 45^\circ}{1 + \tan^2 45^\circ}$ is:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: We know that $\tan 45^\circ = 1$.
Step 2: Substitute the value into the expression: $\frac{1 - (1)^2}{1 + (1)^2} = \frac{1 - 1}{1 + 1}$.
Step 3: $\frac{0}{2} = 0$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: If $\cos A = \frac{1}{2}$, find the value of $\sec A$.

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Recall the reciprocal relationship $\sec A = \frac{1}{\cos A}$.
Step 2: Substitute $\cos A = \frac{1}{2}$ into the formula.
Step 3: $\sec A = \frac{1}{1/2} = 2$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: The distance of the point $P(2, 3)$ from the x-axis is:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: The distance of any point $(x, y)$ from the x-axis is equal to the absolute value of its y-coordinate, $|y|$.
Step 2: For point $P(2, 3)$, the y-coordinate is 3.
Step 3: Therefore, the distance is 3 units.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: Find the distance between the points $(0, 0)$ and $(3, 4)$.

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Use the distance formula: $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$.
Step 2: Substitute the coordinates: $d = \sqrt{(3 - 0)^2 + (4 - 0)^2} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2}$.
Step 3: $d = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: The coordinates of the midpoint of the line segment joining $A(2, 4)$ and $B(4, 6)$ are:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Use the midpoint formula: $M = \left(\frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1 + y_2}{2}\right)$.
Step 2: Substitute the values: $x = \frac{2 + 4}{2} = 3$ and $y = \frac{4 + 6}{2} = 5$.
Step 3: The midpoint is $(3, 5)$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: If the point $P(k, 0)$ divides the line segment joining $A(2, -2)$ and $B(-7, 4)$ in the ratio $1:2$, then $k$ is:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Use the section formula for x-coordinate: $x = \frac{m_1x_2 + m_2x_1}{m_1 + m_2}$.
Step 2: Substitute $m_1=1, m_2=2, x_1=2, x_2=-7$: $k = \frac{1(-7) + 2(2)}{1 + 2}$.
Step 3: $k = \frac{-7 + 4}{3} = \frac{-3}{3} = -1$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics

question_text: The point which lies on the perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining $A(-2, -5)$ and $B(2, 5)$ is:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: The perpendicular bisector of a line segment passes through its midpoint.
Step 2: Find the midpoint of $AB$: $x = \frac{-2 + 2}{2} = 0$ and $y = \frac{-5 + 5}{2} = 0$.
Step 3: The point $(0, 0)$ lies on the bisector.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Introduction to Trigonometry Summary
content:
Trigonometry is the study of relationships between sides and angles of triangles. In Class 10, we focus on right-angled triangles.
  • Trigonometric Ratios: $\sin \theta = \frac{O}{H}$, $\cos \theta = \frac{A}{H}$, $\tan \theta = \frac{O}{A}$.
  • Values: Memorize the table for $0^\circ, 30^\circ, 45^\circ, 60^\circ, 90^\circ$.
  • Identities: The most important one is $\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$.

Subject: Mathematics
title: Coordinate Geometry Summary
content:
Coordinate Geometry allows us to represent points and shapes on a Cartesian plane using $(x, y)$ coordinates.
  • Distance Formula: $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$.
  • Midpoint: $M = (\frac{x_1+x_2}{2}, \frac{y_1+y_2}{2})$.
  • Section Formula: $P = (\frac{m_1x_2+m_2x_1}{m_1+m_2}, \frac{m_1y_2+m_2y_1}{m_1+m_2})$.

question_text: The autotrophic mode of nutrition requires:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Autotrophs (plants) make their own food through photosynthesis.
Step 2: This process requires $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ as raw materials.
Step 3: Chlorophyll (to trap energy) and Sunlight (the energy source) are also essential. Thus, all are required.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Which of the following is the site of complete digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Digestion starts in the mouth and continues in the stomach.
Step 2: However, the small intestine receives secretions from the liver and pancreas.
Step 3: This is where the final breakdown of all food components into their simplest forms occurs.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The process of breakdown of glucose into pyruvate takes place in the:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: The first step of respiration is the breakdown of 6-carbon glucose into 3-carbon pyruvate.
Step 2: This process is called glycolysis.
Step 3: It occurs in the cytoplasm of all living cells, regardless of whether oxygen is present.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Which chamber of the human heart receives oxygenated blood from the lungs?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Oxygenated blood returns from the lungs via pulmonary veins.
Step 2: It first enters the upper left chamber of the heart.
Step 3: This chamber is the left atrium.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The functional unit of filtration in the kidney is the:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: The kidneys contain millions of tiny filtration structures.
Step 2: Each structure consists of a glomerulus and a tubule.
Step 3: These units are called nephrons.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The gap between two neurons is called a:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Neurons are not physically connected to each other.
Step 2: There is a microscopic gap between the axon end of one neuron and the dendrite of the next.
Step 3: Chemical signals (neurotransmitters) cross this gap, which is called a synapse.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The brain is responsible for:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: The forebrain handles complex thinking and memory.
Step 2: The medulla (hindbrain) controls involuntary actions like heartbeat.
Step 3: The cerebellum (hindbrain) maintains posture and balance. Thus, the brain does all these.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Which plant hormone inhibits growth and causes wilting of leaves?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Most plant hormones promote growth or cell division.
Step 2: Abscisic acid ($ABA$) acts as a growth inhibitor.
Step 3: It helps the plant respond to stress and causes the shedding of leaves and fruits.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The hormone responsible for the 'fight or flight' response is:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: In emergency situations, the adrenal glands secrete a hormone directly into the blood.
Step 2: It increases heartbeat, breathing rate, and blood flow to muscles.
Step 3: This hormone is adrenaline.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Insulin is secreted by which gland?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Insulin is crucial for regulating blood sugar levels.
Step 2: It is produced by specialized cells in an organ located behind the stomach.
Step 3: That organ is the pancreas.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology
Biology
title: How do Organisms Reproduce? Summary
content:
Reproduction is the process by which living organisms produce new individuals of their own kind.
  • Asexual Reproduction: Single parent involved (Fission, Budding, Regeneration, Fragmentation, Spore formation).
  • Sexual Reproduction: Involves two parents and fusion of gametes. Increases genetic variation.
  • Plants: Pollination (transfer of pollen) followed by fertilization in the ovary.
  • Humans: Male gamete (Sperm) and Female gamete (Ovum/Egg) fuse to form a zygote.

question_text: Asexual reproduction takes place through budding in:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Budding is where a small outgrowth (bud) develops on the parent body.
Step 2: In yeast (and Hydra), this bud eventually detaches to become a new individual.
Step 3: Amoeba uses binary fission, and Plasmodium uses multiple fission.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The female reproductive part of a flower is the:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Flowers have male and female parts.
Step 2: The stamen is the male part (Anther and Filament).
Step 3: The carpel or pistil is the female part, consisting of Stigma, Style, and Ovary.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a part of the human female reproductive system?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Ovary, Uterus, and Fallopian tubes are essential parts of the female system.
Step 2: Vas deferens is the tube that carries sperm in the male reproductive system.
Step 3: Therefore, it is the correct answer.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Fertilization in humans typically occurs in the:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: The egg is released from the ovary and enters a tube.
Step 2: Sperm travels through the uterus to meet the egg in this tube.
Step 3: The fusion of sperm and egg (fertilization) happens within the Fallopian tube.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Double fertilization is a characteristic of:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: In flowering plants, one male gamete fuses with the egg, and another fuses with two polar nuclei.
Step 2: This unique process involves two fertilization events.
Step 3: Hence, it is called double fertilization.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology
Biology
title: Heredity and Evolution Summary
content:
This chapter deals with the inheritance of traits and how species change over time.
  • Heredity: The transmission of characters from parents to offspring.
  • Mendel's Laws: Based on experiments with pea plants. Traits can be Dominant or Recessive.
  • Sex Determination: In humans, females are XX and males are XY. The father's sperm determines the sex of the child.
  • Evolution: The gradual change in living organisms over time. Evidence comes from fossils, homologous organs (same structure, different function), and analogous organs.

question_text: An example of homologous organs is:

options.A: Our arm and a dog's foreleg
options.B: Our teeth and an elephant's tusks

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Homologous organs have the same basic structural design but perform different functions.
Step 2: Human arms and dog legs share the same bone pattern. Teeth and tusks are both modified teeth.
Step 3: All these pairs share a common evolutionary origin, so all are homologous.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: In evolutionary terms, we have more in common with:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Evolutionary closeness is determined by how recently species shared a common ancestor.
Step 2: All humans belong to the same species (*Homo sapiens*).
Step 3: We have the most in common with other humans, regardless of their nationality.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Who is known as the 'Father of Genetics'?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Charles Darwin is famous for the theory of Natural Selection.
Step 2: Gregor Mendel performed the pioneering experiments on pea plants that explained how traits are inherited.
Step 3: For this work, he is called the Father of Genetics.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: If a round, green seeded pea plant (RRyy) is crossed with wrinkled, yellow seeded pea plant (rrYY), the seeds produced in $F_1$ generation are:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Round (R) is dominant over wrinkled (r), and Yellow (Y) is dominant over green (y).
Step 2: The $F_1$ offspring will have the genotype RrYy.
Step 3: Since dominant traits (Round and Yellow) mask recessive ones, all seeds will be Round and Yellow.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: What is the probability that a male child will be born?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: A mother always provides an X chromosome. A father provides either an X or a Y.
Step 2: The possible combinations are XX (female) or XY (male).
Step 3: Since these two outcomes are equally likely, the probability is 1/2 or 50%.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology
Biology
title: Our Environment Summary
content:
This chapter explores the interaction between living organisms and their environment.
  • Ecosystem: Consists of Biotic (Living) and Abiotic (Non-living) components.
  • Food Chain: Shows the flow of energy. Ten percent law states only 10% of energy is transferred to the next trophic level.
  • Ozone Layer: Protects Earth from harmful UV radiation. Depleted by CFCs (Chlorofluorocarbons).
  • Waste Management: Biodegradable (can be decomposed) vs Non-biodegradable (cannot be decomposed, e.g., Plastic).

question_text: Which of the following are environment-friendly practices?

options.A: Carrying cloth-bags to put purchases in while shopping
options.B: Switching off unnecessary lights and fans
options.C: Walking to school instead of getting your mother to drop you on her scooter

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Reducing plastic use (cloth bags), saving energy (switching off lights), and reducing fuel consumption (walking) are all positive for the environment.
Step 2: These practices help conserve resources and reduce pollution.
Step 3: Therefore, all options are eco-friendly.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: In a food chain, the third trophic level is always occupied by:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: 1st Trophic Level = Producers (Plants).
Step 2: 2nd Trophic Level = Herbivores (Primary consumers).
Step 3: 3rd Trophic Level = Carnivores (Secondary consumers) that eat the herbivores.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Ozone layer depletion is mainly caused by:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: CFCs are used in refrigerators and fire extinguishers.
Step 2: When released into the atmosphere, they break down ozone ($O_3$) molecules in the stratosphere.
Step 3: This leads to the thinning of the ozone layer.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Which of the following is a non-biodegradable waste?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Biodegradable wastes can be broken down by bacteria and fungi.
Step 2: Paper, peels, and cloth are organic and decompose naturally.
Step 3: Plastic is synthetic and cannot be decomposed by natural processes, making it non-biodegradable.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: The percentage of solar energy captured by green plants is about:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: Plants capture solar energy through photosynthesis.
Step 2: However, a very small fraction of the total sunlight falling on leaves is actually converted into chemical energy.
Step 3: This amount is approximately 1%.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Biology
title: Sustainable Management of Natural Resources Summary
content:
This chapter focuses on the wise use of our resources like forests, water, and wildlife.
  • The 5 R's: Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repurpose, Recycle.
  • Forests and Wildlife: Biodiversity hotspots. Importance of local communities in conservation (e.g., Chipko Movement, Amrita Devi Bishnoi).
  • Water Management: Dams (benefits vs social/environmental problems), Water harvesting (traditional methods like Khadins, Taankas).
  • Coal and Petroleum: Non-renewable fossil fuels. Burning them causes global warming.

question_text: The 'Chipko Andolan' was associated with:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: This movement started in a village in Garhwal.
Step 2: Local women hugged trees to prevent them from being cut down by contractors.
Step 3: It became a famous example of community-led forest conservation.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the 5 R's to save the environment?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: The standard 5 R's are: Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repurpose, and Recycle.
Step 2: 'Recover' is sometimes used in waste-to-energy, but it is not part of the core 5 R's taught in Class 10 NCERT.
Step 3: Therefore, it is the odd one out here.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

question_text: Khadins, Ahars, and Bundhis are ancient structures for:

explanations.basic:
Step 1: These are traditional systems used in various parts of India (like Rajasthan and Bihar).
Step 2: They are designed to capture and store rainwater for agriculture and recharging groundwater.
Step 3: Thus, they are water harvesting methods.

question_text: Amrita Devi Bishnoi sacrifice was for the protection of which trees?

explanations.basic:
Step 1: In 1731, Amrita Devi and 363 others sacrificed their lives in Rajasthan.
Step 2: They were protecting trees that are vital to the desert ecosystem.
Step 3: These trees are called Khejri trees.

title: The Rise of Nationalism in Europe Summary
content:
This chapter explores how nationalism emerged as a force in the 19th century, leading to the creation of nation-states in Europe.
  • French Revolution: The first clear expression of nationalism (1789).
  • Napoleon: Introduced the Civil Code (1804), simplifying administration.
  • Unification of Italy: Led by Mazzini, Garibaldi, and Cavour.
  • Unification of Germany: Engineered by Otto von Bismarck ('Blood and Iron' policy).

question_text: Who was responsible for the unification of Germany?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Prussia led the unification movement. Step 2: Bismarck was the architect using the army and bureaucracy. Step 3: He completed it through three wars over seven years.

question_text: Which city is the 'Electronic Capital of India'?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Bengaluru is the IT hub. Step 2: It has numerous software parks. Step 3: It is the center of electronics.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Geography
Geography
title: Lifelines of National Economy Summary
content:
Transportation and communication networks.
  • Roads: Golden Quadrilateral Super Highways.
  • Railways: Principal mode of transport.

question_text: Which organization maintains 'Border Roads'?

explanations.basic: Step 1: BRO builds roads in border areas. Step 2: It was set up in 1960. Step 3: It is vital for defense.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Geography
Civics
title: Power Sharing Summary
content:
This chapter explains why power sharing is desirable in a democracy.
  • Case Studies: Belgium (Successful power sharing) and Sri Lanka (Majoritarianism led to Civil War).
  • Forms of Power Sharing: Horizontal (Organs of Govt), Vertical (Levels of Govt), Social groups, and Political parties.

question_text: Which system of power sharing is also called 'Checks and Balances'?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Horizontal power sharing is among different organs of government: Legislature, Executive, and Judiciary. Step 2: Each organ keeps a check on the others. Step 3: This ensures a balance of power, hence the name.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Civics
Civics
title: Federalism Summary
content:
A system of government in which power is divided between a central authority and constituent units.
  • Key Features: Two or more levels, separate jurisdictions, constitutionally guaranteed.
  • India: A federal country with a three-tier system (Union, State, Panchayati Raj).
  • Decentralization: Transfer of power from Center/State to Local governments (1992 Amendment).

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a subject in the Union List?

explanations.basic: Step 1: The Union List contains subjects of national importance. Step 2: Defense, Foreign Affairs, and Banking are managed by the Central Govt. Step 3: Agriculture is a subject in the State List.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Civics
Civics
title: Gender, Religion and Caste Summary
content:
Examines how social diversities impact politics.
  • Gender: Sexual division of labor, representation in politics.
  • Religion: Communalism and Secularism.
  • Caste: Caste in politics and Politics in caste.

question_text: A person who believes in equal rights and opportunities for women and men is called a:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Feminism is a social and political movement. Step 2: It advocates for gender equality. Step 3: A person supporting this is a feminist.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Civics
Civics
title: Political Parties Summary
content:
The necessity and functions of political parties.
  • Functions: Contesting elections, forming policies, playing the role of opposition.
  • Party Systems: One-party, Two-party, and Multi-party systems.
  • National vs State Parties: Criteria for recognition by Election Commission.

question_text: How many 'National Parties' are currently recognized in India (approx)?

explanations.basic: Step 1: National parties have presence across multiple states. Step 2: They must meet specific vote/seat criteria. Step 3: There are currently around 6-8 recognized national parties (e.g., BJP, INC).
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Civics
Civics
title: Outcomes of Democracy Summary
content:
Assessing the performance of democracy.
  • Accountability: Responsible and legitimate government.
  • Economic Growth: Democracy vs Dictatorship.
  • Social Diversity: Accommodation of social groups.
  • Dignity: Promotion of individual freedom and equality.

question_text: The most basic outcome of democracy should be that it produces a government that is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Democracy is based on the idea of popular sovereignty. Step 2: People have the right to choose their rulers. Step 3: Therefore, the government must be accountable and responsive to their needs.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Civics
Economics
title: Development Summary
content:
This chapter explores what development means for different people and how to compare countries.
  • PCI: Per Capita Income (Total Income / Total Population).
  • HDI: Human Development Index (Life expectancy, Education, Income).
  • Sustainability: Development that does not compromise the needs of future generations.

question_text: What is the main criterion used by the World Bank in classifying different countries?

explanations.basic: Step 1: The World Bank publishes the World Development Reports. Step 2: It uses average income (Per Capita Income) as the primary measure. Step 3: Countries are classified as high, middle, or low income based on this.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Economics
Economics
title: Sectors of the Indian Economy Summary
content:
Classification of economic activities.
  • Primary: Agriculture, Fishing, Mining.
  • Secondary: Manufacturing, Construction.
  • Tertiary: Services (Transport, Banking, IT).
  • Organised vs Unorganised: Terms of employment and government rules.
  • Public vs Private: Ownership of assets.

question_text: Which sector is also known as the 'Service Sector'?

explanations.basic: Step 1: The tertiary sector does not produce goods by itself. Step 2: Instead, it provides support and services to the primary and secondary sectors. Step 3: Examples include transport, communication, and banking.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Economics
Economics
title: Money and Credit Summary
content:
The role of money as a medium of exchange and the importance of credit.
  • Barter System: Double coincidence of wants.
  • Modern Money: Currency and bank deposits.
  • Credit: Formal (Banks, Cooperatives) vs Informal (Moneylenders, Friends) sectors.
  • SHGs: Self-Help Groups for the poor.

question_text: Which institution issues currency notes in India on behalf of the Central Government?

explanations.basic: Step 1: In India, only one institution has the legal authority to issue currency. Step 2: It acts as the central bank of the country. Step 3: That institution is the Reserve Bank of India (RBI).
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Economics
Economics
title: Globalisation and the Indian Economy Summary
content:
The integration of markets and production across countries.
  • MNCs: Multi-National Corporations.
  • FDI: Foreign Direct Investment.
  • WTO: World Trade Organization (Rules for international trade).
  • Impact: Greater choice for consumers, but challenges for small producers.

question_text: A company that owns or controls production in more than one nation is called a:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Globalization is driven by large companies spreading their operations. Step 2: These companies set up factories and offices for production in multiple countries. Step 3: They are called Multi-National Corporations (MNCs).
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Economics
Economics
title: Consumer Rights Summary
content:
Protecting consumers from exploitation.
  • Consumer Movements: Origin and spread.
  • COPRA: Consumer Protection Act (1986).
  • Rights: Right to safety, information, choice, heard, redressal, and consumer education.
  • Standard Marks: ISI, Agmark, Hallmarking.

question_text: When is the 'National Consumers Day' celebrated in India?

explanations.basic: Step 1: The Consumer Protection Act was enacted on this day in 1986. Step 2: Since then, India celebrates it as National Consumers Day. Step 3: The date is 24 December.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

English
title: Dust of Snow Summary
content:
A short poem by Robert Frost that illustrates how a small natural event can change one's mood.
  • The Incident: A crow shakes down dust of snow from a hemlock tree on the poet.
  • The Impact: This simple act saves the rest of the poet's day from being wasted in regret.
  • Symbolism: Hemlock (poisonous) and Crow (bad omen) are used to bring positivity.

question_text: Who is the poet of 'Dust of Snow'?

explanations.basic: Robert Frost is the famous American poet known for this poem.

Subject: English
English
title: Fire and Ice Summary
content:
A poem about the end of the world.
  • Fire: Represents desire and passion.
  • Ice: Represents hatred and coldness.
  • Conclusion: Both are equally powerful and capable of destroying the world.

question_text: What does 'Ice' symbolize in the poem?

explanations.basic: Ice represents the cold, calculating nature of hatred.

Subject: English
English
title: A Triumph of Surgery Summary
content:
Tricky, a small dog, is pampered by his rich mistress Mrs. Pumphrey.
  • The Problem: Tricky becomes hugely fat and listless due to overeating.
  • The Cure: Mr. Herriot (the vet) takes him to the surgery and gives him no food, only water and exercise.
  • Outcome: Tricky recovers completely, which Mrs. Pumphrey calls a 'triumph of surgery'.

question_text: Who was Tricky?

explanations.basic: Tricky was the pampered pet dog of Mrs. Pumphrey.

Subject: English
English
title: The Thief's Story Summary
content:
A 15-year-old thief (Hari Singh) befriends a kind writer named Anil.
  • The Trust: Anil teaches Hari to read, write, and cook.
  • The Theft: Hari steals Anil's money but returns it because he realizes the value of education and Anil's trust.
  • Transformation: Hari decides to leave his criminal life for a better future.

question_text: What was Anil's profession?

explanations.basic: Anil wrote for magazines to make a living.

Subject: English
English
title: The Necklace Summary
content:
Matilda Loisel, a pretty but dissatisfied woman, borrows a diamond necklace for a ball.
  • The Loss: She loses the necklace and spends 10 years in poverty to replace it.
  • The Irony: She later discovers that the original necklace was fake (cost only 500 francs).

question_text: How many years did the Loisels spend repaying the debt?

explanations.basic: They spent 10 long years of hard labor and poverty to pay off the 36,000 francs.

Subject: English
English
title: Bholi Summary
content:
The story of Sulekha (Bholi), who is neglected due to her looks and stammering.
  • The Teacher: Her school teacher encourages her and gives her confidence.
  • The Decision: Bholi refuses to marry Bishamber (a greedy lame man) and decides to serve her parents and teach in the school.

question_text: What was Bholi's real name?

explanations.basic: Bholi was called so because she was considered a simpleton, but her real name was Sulekha.

Subject: English
Hindi
title: Bade Bhai Sahab Summary
content:
This famous story by Munshi Premchand explores the relationship between two brothers.
  • The Elder Brother: Serious, hard-working, but fails his exams.
  • The Younger Brother: Playful, talented, and passes his exams easily.
  • The Lesson: The elder brother explains that experience and character are more important than just school grades.

question_text: Who is the author of 'Bade Bhai Sahab'?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Premchand is known as the 'Katha Samrat'. Step 2: This story is one of his masterpieces. Step 3: It depicts human psychology and traditional values.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Hindi
Hindi
title: Diary ka ek Panna Summary
content:
A real diary entry by Sitaram Seksaria about the events in Calcutta on 26 January 1931.
  • The Context: The first anniversary of the independence declaration.
  • The Conflict: The British government tried to stop the celebrations with force.
  • The Result: Thousands of people joined the protest, showing the power of national unity.

question_text: On which date did the events in 'Diary ka ek Panna' take place?

explanations.basic: Step 1: This date was celebrated as the first independence day (Purna Swaraj). Step 2: The author describes the bravery of Calcutta's people. Step 3: It was a historic day in the freedom struggle.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Hindi
Hindi
title: Tantara-Vamiro Katha Summary
content:
A folk tale from the Andaman and Nicobar Islands about a tragic love story.
  • The Characters: Tantara (strong, helpful) and Vamiro (a girl from another village).
  • The Taboo: Marriage between different villages was forbidden.
  • The End: Their sacrifice leads to the breaking of this tradition, uniting the islands.

question_text: Which islands are associated with the 'Tantara-Vamiro Katha'?

explanations.basic: Step 1: This story belongs to the tribal traditions of India. Step 2: It explains why Little Andaman and Car Nicobar are separated. Step 3: It is set in the Andaman and Nicobar group of islands.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Hindi
Hindi
Topic: Ab Kahan Dusre ke Dukh se Dukhi Hone Wale
title: Ab Kahan Dusre ke Dukh se Dukhi Hone Wale Summary
content:
Nida Fazli highlights the increasing selfishness in human nature and the loss of empathy for nature and other creatures.
  • Historical Examples: Prophet Solomon (Suleman) and his kindness to ants.
  • Environmental Change: How humans have destroyed nature, leading to a loss of peace.
  • The Message: We should coexist with all living beings and care for the environment.

question_text: Who is the author of this chapter?

explanations.basic: Step 1: The author is a famous poet and writer. Step 2: He writes about social and emotional issues. Step 3: Nida Fazli is the correct author.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

chapter: Ab Kahan Dusre ke Dukh se Dukhi Hone Wale
Subject: Hindi
Topic: Ab Kahan Dusre ke Dukh se Dukhi Hone Wale

Subject: Hindi
title: Patjhad mein Tooti Pattiyan Summary
content:
Consists of two short stories: 'Ginni ka Sona' and 'Zen ki Den'.
  • Ginni ka Sona: Explains why pure gold (ideals) is different from mixed gold (practicality).
  • Zen ki Den: A story from Japan about the importance of 'living in the present' through tea ceremony.

question_text: 'Zen ki Den' story is set in which country?

explanations.basic: Step 1: The author visits a country where people are under high stress. Step 2: He participates in a traditional tea ceremony. Step 3: This ceremony is a key part of Japanese culture.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Hindi
Hindi
title: Kartoos Summary
content:
A play about the bravery of Wazir Ali, a freedom fighter who challenged the British East India Company.
  • The Setting: A forest camp where British officers are trying to capture Wazir Ali.
  • The Encounter: Wazir Ali enters the camp alone, disguised as a rider, and takes cartridges (kartoos) from the officer.
  • The Twist: He leaves without being caught, impressing even his enemy with his courage.

question_text: Who is the main hero of the play 'Kartoos'?

explanations.basic: Step 1: The story is about an Indian rebel who fought against the British. Step 2: He was once the Nawab of Awadh. Step 3: His name was Wazir Ali.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Hindi
Hindi
title: Harihar Kaka Summary
content:
A poignant story about an elderly man, Harihar Kaka, who becomes a victim of greed by his family and the local temple (Mahant).
  • The Property: Kaka owns 15 bighas of land, which everyone wants.
  • The Conflict: Both his brothers and the Mahant use force and deceit to get his property.
  • The Outcome: Kaka becomes silent and disillusioned with the world, realizing that money has destroyed human relationships.

question_text: How much land did Harihar Kaka own?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Kaka had no children, so his land was his only security. Step 2: His brothers and the temple priest both eyed his property. Step 3: The exact measurement mentioned in the story is 15 bighas.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Hindi
Hindi
title: Sapnon ke se Din Summary
content:
An autobiographical account of the author's childhood school days.
  • School Experience: Memories of strict teachers, playing games, and the fear of exams.
  • Pritam Chand: The strict PT teacher who was feared by all students but loved his birds.
  • Nostalgia: Reflections on how childhood dreams and memories stay with us forever.

question_text: Who was the strict PT teacher in 'Sapnon ke se Din'?

explanations.basic: Step 1: The author describes a teacher who was very disciplined. Step 2: He used to punish students severely for mistakes. Step 3: His name was Master Pritam Chand.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Hindi
Hindi
title: Topi Shukla Summary
content:
A touching story about the friendship between Topi (a Hindu boy) and Iffan (a Muslim boy).
  • The Bond: Their friendship transcends religious boundaries.
  • Iffan's Grandmother: Topi felt a special connection with Iffan's grandmother, whose love was pure and unconditional.
  • Loss: The story highlights the pain of separation and the innocence of childhood friendship.

question_text: Who was Topi's best friend?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Topi came from a traditional Hindu family. Step 2: He found emotional comfort in a Muslim friend's house. Step 3: That friend was Iffan.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Mathematics
title: Number Systems Summary
content:
This chapter introduces irrational numbers and real numbers.
  • Rational: $p/q$ form.
  • Irrational: Non-terminating non-repeating (e.g., $\sqrt{2}$).
  • Real: All rational + irrational.

question_text: Every rational number is a:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Real numbers include all rational and irrational numbers. Step 2: Every rational number is part of the real number set.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Polynomials Summary
content:
Study of expressions like $p(x) = a_nx^n + ...$.
  • Zeroes: Roots of the equation.
  • Identities: $(a+b)^3$, $a^3+b^3+c^3-3abc$, etc.

question_text: The coefficient of $x^2$ in $2 + x^2 + x$ is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Locate the $x^2$ term. Step 2: The multiplier is 1.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Coordinate Geometry Summary
content:
Cartesian system with X and Y axes.
  • Abscissa: x-coordinate.
  • Ordinate: y-coordinate.

question_text: The distance of point (4, 3) from X-axis is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Distance from X-axis is the y-coordinate. Step 2: The y-coordinate is 3.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Linear Equations in Two Variables Summary
content:
Equations like $ax+by+c=0$.
  • Solutions: Infinite.
  • Graph: Straight line.

question_text: The point of the form $(a, a)$ always lies on:

explanations.basic: Step 1: In $(a, a)$, $x = y$. Step 2: This corresponds to the equation $y=x$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Introduction to Euclid’s Geometry Summary
content:
Basic definitions, axioms, and postulates.
  • Axioms: Self-evident truths.
  • Postulates: Geometric assumptions.

question_text: A point has how many dimensions?

explanations.basic: Step 1: A point is a position with no length, width, or height. Step 2: Thus, it has 0 dimensions.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Lines and Angles Summary
content:
Study of angles formed by intersecting and parallel lines.
  • Linear Pair: Sum is $180^\circ$.
  • Parallel Lines: Alternate interior angles are equal.

question_text: An angle which is greater than $180^\circ$ but less than $360^\circ$ is called:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Reflex angles are between $180^\circ$ and $360^\circ$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Triangles Summary
content:
Congruence of triangles and properties.
  • Congruence Rules: SAS, ASA, SSS, RHS.
  • Inequalities: Side opposite to greater angle is longer.

question_text: In $\triangle ABC$, if $AB=AC$ and $\angle B=50^\circ$, then $\angle C$ is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Angles opposite to equal sides are equal. Step 2: Since $AB=AC$, $\angle C = \angle B = 50^\circ$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Quadrilaterals Summary
content:
Properties of parallelograms and the mid-point theorem.
  • Parallelogram: Opposite sides and angles are equal.
  • Mid-point Theorem: Joins midpoints of two sides.

question_text: The sum of all angles of a quadrilateral is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: A quadrilateral can be split into two triangles. Step 2: $2 \times 180^\circ = 360^\circ$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Circles Summary
content:
Theorems related to chords and angles.
  • Equal chords: Subtend equal angles at center.
  • Cyclic Quad: Opposite angles sum to $180^\circ$.

question_text: The longest chord of a circle is its:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Chords pass through the circle. Step 2: The chord passing through the center is the longest.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Heron’s Formula Summary
content:
Area of a triangle with sides $a, b, c$.
  • Semi-perimeter: $s = (a+b+c)/2$.
  • Area: $\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$.

question_text: Find area of triangle with sides 3, 4, 5.

explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Surface Areas and Volumes Summary
content:
Formulas for 3D shapes.
  • Sphere: $4\pi r^2$ (SA), $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ (Vol).
  • Cone: $\pi rl$ (CSA), $\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2h$ (Vol).

question_text: Volume of a cylinder is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Base area $\times$ height. Step 2: $\pi r^2 \cdot h$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
title: Statistics Summary
content:
Data collection and measures of central tendency.
  • Mean: Sum of values / Number of values.
  • Median: Middle value.
  • Mode: Most frequent value.

question_text: The mean of first five natural numbers is:

explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

title: Matter in Our Surroundings Summary
content:
Matter is made of particles and exists in three states.
  • States: Solid, Liquid, Gas.
  • Latent Heat: Energy required to change state without changing temperature.
  • Evaporation: Surface phenomenon causing cooling.

question_text: The boiling point of water at sea level is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius under standard atmospheric pressure.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Chemistry
Chemistry
title: Is Matter Around Us Pure Summary
content:
Classification of matter into pure substances and mixtures.
  • Solutions: Homogeneous mixtures.
  • Suspensions: Heterogeneous mixtures.
  • Colloids: Tyndall effect.
  • Separation: Distillation, Centrifugation, Chromatography.

question_text: A mixture of oil and water can be separated by:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Oil and water are immiscible liquids. Step 2: A separating funnel is used to separate them based on density.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Chemistry
Chemistry

content:
Laws of chemical combination and the concept of mole.
  • Law of Conservation of Mass: Mass is neither created nor destroyed.
  • Mole: $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles.
  • Atomic Mass: Relative mass compared to C-12.

question_text: The value of Avogadro's constant is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Avogadro's number is the number of constituent particles per mole of a substance.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Biology
title: The Fundamental Unit of Life Summary
content:
Introduction to cells.
  • Discovery: Robert Hooke (1665).
  • Organelles: Nucleus, Mitochondria (Powerhouse), Ribosomes (Protein), Lysosomes (Suicide bags).
  • Plant vs Animal: Cell wall and vacuoles.

question_text: Which organelle is known as the powerhouse of the cell?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Mitochondria produce ATP, which is the energy currency of the cell.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology

title: Tissues Summary
content:
Group of cells performing a specific function.
  • Plant: Meristematic and Permanent (Simple, Complex).
  • Animal: Epithelial, Connective, Muscular, Nervous.
  • Xylem/Phloem: Transport of water and food.

question_text: Which tissue transports food in plants?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Xylem transports water. Step 2: Phloem transports sugars from leaves to other parts.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Biology
Science
title: Motion Summary
content:
Description of motion in a straight line.
  • Distance vs Displacement: Scalar vs Vector.
  • Speed vs Velocity: Rate of change of position.
  • Acceleration: Change in velocity over time.
  • Equations: $v=u+at$, $s=ut+1/2at^2$, $v^2=u^2+2as$.

question_text: The rate of change of displacement is:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Velocity is displacement divided by time.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

content:
Study of subatomic particles and atomic models.
  • Particles: Electrons (J.J. Thomson), Protons (E. Goldstein), Neutrons (J. Chadwick).
  • Models: Thomson's Plum Pudding, Rutherford's Gold Foil, Bohr's Model.
  • Valency: Combining capacity of an atom.
  • Isotopes: Same atomic number, different mass number.

explanations.basic: Step 1: Neutrons were discovered by James Chadwick in 1932.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Classification of life forms.

explanations.basic: Step 1: Robert Whittaker proposed the 5-kingdom system in 1969.

Science
title: Force and Laws of Motion Summary
content:
Understanding force and Newton's laws.
  • First Law (Inertia): Object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a force.
  • Second Law ($F=ma$): Force equals rate of change of momentum.
  • Third Law: Every action has an equal and opposite reaction.

question_text: Which law of motion is also known as the Law of Inertia?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Newton's first law states that an object resists changes to its state of motion, which is the definition of inertia.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Science
Science
title: Gravitation Summary
content:
Universal law of gravitation.
  • Universal Law: $F = G rac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$.
  • Free Fall: Acceleration due to gravity ($g = 9.8 m/s^2$).
  • Mass vs Weight: Mass is constant, weight ($W=mg$) varies with gravity.
  • Archimedes' Principle: Buoyant force equals weight of fluid displaced.

question_text: The value of $g$ on the surface of the Earth is approximately:

explanations.basic: Step 1: The standard acceleration due to gravity on Earth is $9.8 m/s^2$.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

Subject: Science
Science
title: Work and Energy Summary
content:
Concepts of work, power, and energy.
  • Work ($W=Fd$): Done when force causes displacement.
  • Kinetic Energy ($1/2 mv^2$): Energy of motion.
  • Potential Energy ($mgh$): Energy of position.
  • Law of Conservation: Energy can only be transformed, not created or destroyed.

question_text: What is the SI unit of work?

explanations.basic: Step 1: Work is measured in Joules (J). One Joule is one Newton-meter.
explanations.advanced: Excellent job! You have a solid grasp of this concept.

  • Characteristics: Frequency (Pitch), Amplitude (Loudness), Speed.
  • SONAR: Sound Navigation and Ranging.

question_text: Sound travels fastest in:

explanations.basic: Step 1: Sound waves travel faster in solids because particles are closer together.

question_text: What is the name of the experiment that led to the discovery of the nucleus?

explanations.advanced: In this experiment, Ernest Rutherford bombarded a thin gold foil with alpha particles and observed the scattering of the particles, which led him to propose the existence of a small, dense nucleus at the center of the atom.

question_text: What is the atomic number of an element?

options.C: The number of electrons in the outermost shell
options.D: The number of neutrons in the nucleus

options.D: To suppress the voices of minorities
